title: The Iron Saint's Face

activity.name: Le Suquet — Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance Facade Investigation
activity.address: 1 Place de la Castre, 06400 Cannes, France

narrative_intro:
The climb up Le Suquet feels like leaving one century for another. The narrow streets tighten around you — limestone walls, shuttered windows, laundry lines casting shadows like prison bars. Your calves burn. Good. Pain means you're still ahead of whoever else is climbing.

Le Pharmacien's coordinates didn't lead to a building. They led to a face. Specifically, to the carved stone facade of Notre-Dame d'Espérance, the church that has watched over Cannes since 1648 — thirty years before the Man in the Iron Mask was moved from Île Sainte-Marguerite. The cipher map's second reference point isn't a street or a monument. It's an architectural detail on this church. A saint's face. According to Le Pharmacien's fragment, the jailer encoded the next bearing using the direction a particular carved figure is looking.

But your phone buzzes as you climb. It's Noor. She sounds different now — urgent, the intellectual coolness cracking. She says Marco Delvecchio just contacted the buyer's network directly. She intercepted the message. He's offering to authenticate the reliquary himself — cutting you out entirely. He's telling them you're compromised. She wants to know: did you share anything with Capitaine Morin?

You haven't even caught your breath.
instruction: Climb to the Place de la Castre at the top of Le Suquet. Find the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance. Photograph the carved stone figures above the main entrance — focus on any saint or figure whose face is turned to one side rather than facing forward. Send the photo to Noor, who claims she can cross-reference the figure's gaze direction with the cipher map fragment. But be aware: Vera will also contact you, and her instructions will contradict Noor's.
player_action: Photograph the carved stone figures on the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance, specifically any figure whose head or gaze is turned to one side. Examine the direction the figure faces and report it (left, right, upward) to the characters who request it.

gps_trigger.content_brief: A high-resolution scan of a 1698 prison inventory from Île Sainte-Marguerite appears in the vault. One entry is circled in red digital ink: 'Une figure de saint, en pierre, transportée de l'île à la chapelle du Suquet, sur ordre du gouverneur.' A stone saint figure, transported from the island to the Le Suquet chapel on the governor's order. Le Pharmacien's annotation reads: 'The jailer didn't draw a map. He built one. Into the city itself. The saint's gaze is the compass bearing. —Ph.' Below is a partial overlay showing how three reference points (the Carlton's original site, the port, and this church) form a triangle whose center falls inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde, 90 meters from where you stand.

camera_prompt: The player photographs the stone carvings on the facade of Notre-Dame d'Espérance. AI should identify carved figures, saints, or reliefs above the entrance. Narratively interpret the direction any figure's gaze points as a compass bearing that, combined with previous reference points (the Carlton and the Vieux Port), triangulates a specific room inside the nearby Musée des Explorations du Monde. If the photo shows the church clearly, confirm the bearing. If unclear or wrong building, have Noor express doubt and ask for another angle.

tension.description: Marco has gone directly to the buyer's network, offering to replace the player as authenticator. Noor reveals this but her motives are suspect — she may be manufacturing urgency to control the player's next move. Simultaneously, Vera contacts the player with a contradictory instruction: do NOT send the photo to Noor, send it only to her. The player must choose who to trust with the critical compass bearing, knowing that sharing it determines who reaches the Musée first.
tension.resolution: The player sends the photo to one or both parties. If they send to Noor, she provides a detailed but slightly altered bearing (protecting her own interest in reaching the documents first). If they send to Vera, she gives the accurate bearing but reveals nothing about why she needs it so badly. If they send to both, the contradictions between their interpretations become visible — a clue that one of them is lying. The story continues regardless, but the player's information flow determines alliance positioning for the climax.

character_interactions.trigger: GPS arrival at Place de la Castre
character_interactions.phone_version: Noor calls immediately, voice tight with controlled anger: 'I intercepted a message from Delvecchio to the buyer's broker. He's telling them you've been talking to the police. He's offering himself as replacement authenticator. That snake — he did the same thing in Istanbul.' She catches herself. Pauses. 'Forget I said that. Listen, I need you to photograph the carved figures on the church facade. My father's research predicted that the jailer used architectural landmarks as cipher points. The direction the saint faces gives us the final bearing. Send me the photo. Only me. Can you do that?' If the player asks about Istanbul or Marco, she deflects: 'Later. We're losing time. The bearing first.'

rayban_version.script: Noor's voice comes through the glasses, clipped and urgent: 'Delvecchio just betrayed you to the buyer's network. He's offering to replace you. I intercepted the message. I need you to look at the church facade — the carved figures above the entrance. Find the one whose face is turned. That's our compass point. My father spent years on this. Describe what you see.'

camera_mode.purpose: Player looks at the church facade through Ray-Ban camera; AI identifies carved figures and their gaze direction, feeding this into Noor's real-time analysis

character_interactions.phone_version: A text from Vera, all caps: 'DO NOT SEND THAT PHOTO TO NOOR. She is not who she says she is. Her father didn't just study the Iron Mask — he was hired by the same network that bought the reliquary. His research was funded by laundered money from the 2013 heist. She's not seeking academic vindication. She's completing a transaction her father started. Send the photo to me. I will explain everything at the next location. You have earned that much.' A pause. Then: 'I know I'm asking you to trust me. I know I haven't given you enough reason. But Noor has given you less. Think about what she's actually told you versus what she's asked you to do.'

rayban_version.script: Vera's message appears as text overlay in the glasses: 'STOP. Do not share the facade photo with Noor. Her father was funded by the same network behind the Carlton heist. She is finishing his transaction, not his research. Send the bearing to me. I will explain at the museum. You have earned that.'

character_interactions.trigger: Player sends photo to either Noor or Vera (or 3 minutes after arrival if no action)
character_interactions.phone_version: A terse audio message from Morin: 'This is Capitaine Morin. I'll be brief because I don't know who else is listening. Delvecchio was just seen at the Palais des Festivals talking to a man matching the description of the buyer's broker. He had documents. If your so-called colleagues are telling you to rush, ask yourself who benefits from you not stopping to think. The yacht hasn't moved. Its engine hasn't started. Someone is lying to you about the clock. I don't need your photo. I need you to keep your eyes open at the museum. Morin out.'

rayban_version.script: Morin's voice, low and steady through the glasses: 'Capitaine Morin. Delvecchio was seen at the Palais talking to the buyer's broker. He had documents. Your friends are pushing you to rush — ask who benefits. The yacht hasn't moved. Someone is lying about the deadline. I don't need your photo. I need your eyes at the museum. Morin out.'

verification.target: Carved stone figures on the facade of Église Notre-Dame d'Espérance, focusing on gaze direction
verification.success_condition: Photo clearly shows the church facade with identifiable carved figures or architectural reliefs. AI can determine which direction a figure's head or gaze is oriented.
verification.success_reaction: Whichever character receives the photo responds with a triangulation analysis. The three reference points — the Carlton (Step 1), the Vieux Port (Step 3), and the saint's gaze bearing — converge on a specific wing of the Musée des Explorations du Monde. The player receives +15 XP and an exclusive vault document: a superimposed map showing the triangulation with a red circle marking the exact room. The character who received the photo addresses the player with new respect: 'You see it now, don't you? The jailer hid the answer in plain sight for three hundred years. And you just read it.'
verification.failure_fallback: If the photo is unclear or of the wrong building, Noor (or Vera) expresses frustration but pivots: 'The angle isn't right, but it doesn't matter — Le Pharmacien's overlay already narrowed it to the museum's west gallery. We go with what we have. Move to the Musée now.' The player still progresses but without the precise room location, making the next step slightly harder. Dimitri sends a mocking voice note: 'Couldn't find the church? It's the big stone thing. With the cross. On top of the hill you're standing on.'
verification.timeout_reaction: After 5 minutes of inaction, Dimitri sends a message: 'Standing on a hilltop staring at a church. Very spiritual. Very useless. Delvecchio is already at the Palais selling your credentials to the buyer. Move or be moved.' Simultaneously, Vera sends coordinates for the museum directly: 'Forget the bearing. I have enough. Meet me at the next point. We're out of time — or we're not. Either way, standing still is the one thing that definitely doesn't help.'

unlock_message: A 1698 prison inventory document has appeared in your vault. Le Pharmacien has marked an entry about a stone saint transported from Île Sainte-Marguerite to Le Suquet. The jailer didn't just draw a map — he built it into the architecture of Cannes itself.
